title: Meta-regression with study-level covariates
text: simulate 40 studies true treatment effect depends linearly covariate called dose. Higher doses produce negative treatment effect: θi∼𝒩(μθ+β⋅dosei,σθ2)\theta_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_\theta + \beta \cdot \text{dose}_i,\; \sigma_\theta^2) covariates argument takes data frame one row per study, beta_cov numeric vector regression coefficients (one per covariate column). , unit increase dose shifts raw treatment effect -0.04.

code: fit <- meta_did(   summary_data = did_data,   covariates   = ~ dose,   seed         = 8153 )  print(fit) #> Bayesian meta-analysis (metadid) #> Studies: DiD = 40 | RCT = 0 | Pre-Post = 0 | DiD (change only) = 0  #> Population treatment effect: -0.538  90% CI [-0.560, -0.516] #> Covariate coefficients: #>   dose: -0.081  90% CI [-0.105, -0.057] #>   (covariates were mean-centered; treatment_effect_mean is the effect at covariate means)

title: Meta-regression with study-level covariates
text: useful diagnostic plot observed study-level treatment effects covariate overlay estimated regression line uncertainty:  points naive study-level estimates, solid line posterior median regression 90% credible band, dashed line true data-generating relationship.
code: # Compute naive normalised effect per study naive_effects <- did_data |>   mutate(     norm = mean_pre_control,     naive_effect = (mean_post_treatment - mean_pre_treatment) / norm -                    (mean_post_control - mean_pre_control) / norm   )  # Posterior draws for regression line beta_draws <- as.numeric(   fit$fit$draws("beta_cov", format = "draws_matrix") ) intercept_draws <- as.numeric(   fit$fit$draws("treatment_effect_mean", format = "draws_matrix") )  # Covariates were centered; reconstruct on original dose scale dose_center <- fit$cov_centers[["dose"]] dose_grid <- seq(min(dose), max(dose), length.out = 100)  # Each draw gives a line: intercept + beta * (dose - center) line_df <- expand.grid(   dose = dose_grid,   draw = seq_along(beta_draws) ) |>   mutate(     fitted = intercept_draws[draw] + beta_draws[draw] * (dose - dose_center)   ) |>   group_by(dose) |>   summarise(     median = median(fitted),     lo     = quantile(fitted, 0.05),     hi     = quantile(fitted, 0.95),     .groups = "drop"   )  # True regression line on the normalised scale true_line_df <- data.frame(dose = range(dose)) |>   mutate(true_effect = (-0.15 + -0.04 * dose) / 0.45)  ggplot() +   geom_ribbon(data = line_df, aes(x = dose, ymin = lo, ymax = hi),               alpha = 0.2) +   geom_line(data = line_df, aes(x = dose, y = median)) +   geom_line(data = true_line_df, aes(x = dose, y = true_effect),             linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.8) +   geom_point(data = naive_effects, aes(x = dose, y = naive_effect),              alpha = 0.6) +   labs(x = "Dose", y = "Normalised treatment effect") +   theme_minimal()

title: Model details and identification
text: metadid assumes every study — regardless design — arises common latent difference--differences () data-generating process. study ii, outcomes control group follow (Yi,c,preYi,c,post)∼𝒩[(αiαi+βi),(σi,c,pre2ρi,cσi,c,preσi,c,postρi,cσi,c,preσi,c,postσi,c,post2)], \begin{pmatrix} Y_{,c,\mathrm{pre}} \\ Y_{,c,\mathrm{post}} \end{pmatrix} \sim \mathcal{N} \left[ \begin{pmatrix} \alpha_i \\ \alpha_i + \beta_i \end{pmatrix} , \begin{pmatrix} \sigma^2_{,c,\mathrm{pre}} & \rho_{,c}\,\sigma_{,c,\mathrm{pre}}\,\sigma_{,c,\mathrm{post}} \\ \rho_{,c}\,\sigma_{,c,\mathrm{pre}}\,\sigma_{,c,\mathrm{post}} & \sigma^2_{,c,\mathrm{post}} \end{pmatrix} \right], outcomes treatment group follow (Yi,t,preYi,t,post)∼𝒩[(αi+γiαi+γi+βi+θi),(σi,t,pre2ρi,tσi,t,preσi,t,postρi,tσi,t,preσi,t,postσi,t,post2)]. \begin{pmatrix} Y_{,t,\mathrm{pre}} \\ Y_{,t,\mathrm{post}} \end{pmatrix} \sim \mathcal{N} \left[ \begin{pmatrix} \alpha_i + \gamma_i \\ \alpha_i + \gamma_i + \beta_i + \theta_i \end{pmatrix} , \begin{pmatrix} \sigma^2_{,t,\mathrm{pre}} & \rho_{,t}\,\sigma_{,t,\mathrm{pre}}\,\sigma_{,t,\mathrm{post}} \\ \rho_{,t}\,\sigma_{,t,\mathrm{pre}}\,\sigma_{,t,\mathrm{post}} & \sigma^2_{,t,\mathrm{post}} \end{pmatrix} \right]. parameters : αi\alpha_i: baseline mean control group βi\beta_i: time trend shared across groups γi\gamma_i: baseline difference treatment control groups θi\theta_i: study-specific treatment effect (estimand) ρi,c\rho_{,c}, ρi,t\rho_{,t}: pre/post correlations within group σi,g,t\sigma_{,g,t}: marginal standard deviations key identifying assumption , absent treatment, treatment group followed time trend βi\beta_i control group (.e., parallel trends assumption).

(\mu_{,t,\mathrm{post}} - \mu_{,t,\mathrm{pre}}) - (\mu_{,c,\mathrm{post}} - \mu_{,c,\mathrm{pre}}) = (\beta_i + \theta_i) - \beta_i = \theta_i. time trend βi\beta_i cancels, baseline difference γi\gamma_i cancels, leaving θi\theta_i cleanly identified. studies anchor entire model.

However, pre-treatment levels observed, studies anchor normalisation baseline (see ).

what: RCT studies: treatment effect confounded with baseline differences
title: Model details and identification
text: RCT studies observe post-treatment outcomes arms. expected difference arms μi,t,post−μi,c,post=(αi+γi+βi+θi)−(αi+βi)=γi+θi. \mu_{,t,\mathrm{post}} - \mu_{,c,\mathrm{post}} = (\alpha_i + \gamma_i + \beta_i + \theta_i) - (\alpha_i + \beta_i) = \gamma_i + \theta_i. Without pre-treatment data, θi\theta_i confounded baseline difference γi\gamma_i. Randomisation makes γi\gamma_i small expectation, model separate two data single RCT alone.

what: Pre-post studies: treatment effect confounded with time trends
title: Model details and identification
text: Pre-post studies observe treatment arm time points. expected change time μi,t,post−μi,t,pre=(αi+γi+βi+θi)−(αi+γi)=βi+θi. \mu_{,t,\mathrm{post}} - \mu_{,t,\mathrm{pre}} = (\alpha_i + \gamma_i + \beta_i + \theta_i) - (\alpha_i + \gamma_i) = \beta_i + \theta_i. Without control arm, θi\theta_i confounded time trend βi\beta_i.

what: Why model the baseline rather than divide by the sample mean
title: Model details and identification
text: naive plug-approach — dividing observed means SDs sample control-pre mean fitting — treats baseline known exactly, ignoring sampling uncertainty. Jensen’s inequality ratio sample means equal ratio population means; plug-estimator picks finite-sample bias scales variance baseline estimator. Modelling baseline latent parameter inside joint posterior correctly propagates uncertainty, eliminating Jensen contribution bias producing credible intervals fractional effect right shape. cost computational: model carries one additional latent parameter per study. wide uniform prior parameter upper bound auto-computed R 100× maximum observed baseline mean (overridable via set_priors(baseline_per_study = uniform(0, X))), effectively flat data-supported region.

title: Bayesian Meta-Analysis Across Study Designs
text: best case, every study provides full four-cell summary statistics (pre/post × control/treatment). gives model maximum information per study: Now suppose, underlying data, third studies provide full information. Another third RCTs (post-treatment outcomes ), remaining third pre-post studies (treatment arm ):
code: all_did <- as_summary_did(sim)  fit_all_did <- meta_did(   summary_data  = all_did,   seed          = 42,   iter_warmup   = 2000,   iter_sampling = 2000,   adapt_delta   = 0.95 )  print(fit_all_did) #> Bayesian meta-analysis (metadid) #> Studies: DiD = 30 | RCT = 0 | Pre-Post = 0 | DiD (change only) = 0  #> Population treatment effect: -0.323  90% CI [-0.349, -0.296] study_ids <- unique(sim$study_id) true_params <- attr(sim, "true_params")  sim_did <- sim |> filter(study_id %in% study_ids[1:10]) sim_rct <- sim |> filter(study_id %in% study_ids[11:20]) sim_pp  <- sim |> filter(study_id %in% study_ids[21:30])  attr(sim_did, "true_params") <- true_params |> filter(study_id %in% study_ids[1:10]) attr(sim_rct, "true_params") <- true_params |> filter(study_id %in% study_ids[11:20]) attr(sim_pp, "true_params")  <- true_params |> filter(study_id %in% study_ids[21:30])  mixed <- bind_rows(   as_summary_did(sim_did),   as_summary_rct(sim_rct),   as_summary_pp(sim_pp) )  fit_mixed <- meta_did(   summary_data  = mixed,   seed          = 42,   iter_warmup   = 2000,   iter_sampling = 2000,   adapt_delta   = 0.95 )  print(fit_mixed) #> Bayesian meta-analysis (metadid) #> Studies: DiD = 10 | RCT = 10 | Pre-Post = 10 | DiD (change only) = 0  #> Population treatment effect: -0.326  90% CI [-0.355, -0.297]

title: Bayesian Meta-Analysis Across Study Designs
text: fits recover true normalised effect (−0.333, dashed line). mixed-design posterior slightly wider, reflecting information lost two-thirds studies provide incomplete data — difference modest.  key takeaway: assuming common latent structure, model borrows strength across designs. RCT pre-post studies contribute meaningful information treatment effect, even though observe less underlying process full study.
code: draws_did <- as.numeric(   fit_all_did$fit$draws("treatment_effect_mean", format = "draws_matrix") ) draws_mix <- as.numeric(   fit_mixed$fit$draws("treatment_effect_mean", format = "draws_matrix") )  comp_df <- data.frame(   value = c(draws_did, draws_mix),   scenario = rep(c("All DiD (30 studies)", "Mixed designs (10 DiD + 10 RCT + 10 PP)"),                  each = length(draws_did)) )  ggplot(comp_df, aes(x = value, fill = scenario)) +   geom_density(alpha = 0.4) +   geom_vline(xintercept = -0.15 / 0.45, linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.8) +   annotate("text", x = -0.15 / 0.45 + 0.003, y = Inf, label = "True effect",            hjust = 0, vjust = 1.5, size = 3.5) +   labs(x = "Population treatment effect (normalised)", y = "Density", fill = NULL) +   theme_minimal() +   theme(legend.position = "bottom")

title: Fit a Bayesian meta-analysis model across study designs — meta_did
text: summary_data data frame one row per study containing summary statistics. Must include columns study_id design. See validate_summary_data() full column specification per design. Valid designs: "", "did_change", "rct", "pp". individual_data data frame long format one row per observation. Must include columns study_id, design, group, time, value. Valid designs: "", "rct", "pp". study_id may appear summary_data individual_data. normalise Logical. TRUE (default), population-level effects (treatment_effect_mean, time_trend_mean, baseline_difference_mean) expressed fractions treatment-arm pre-treatment baseline. Stan receives raw data unchanged performs normalisation internally via per-study latent baseline parameters. FALSE, per-study latent baseline fit; population-level effects pooled absolute (user-units) scale instead. Equivalent legacy normalise_by_baseline = FALSE behaviour. baseline_latent_arm Character. Advanced. used normalise = TRUE. Determines arm's pre-treatment baseline per-study latent parameter (one wide data-vague uniform prior); arm's baseline derived via hierarchical baseline-difference parameter. One : "treatment" (default): treatment-arm pre-baseline latent, informed directly mean_pre_treatment observations. "control": control-arm pre-baseline latent, informed directly mean_pre_control observations. choice change canonical scale effects reported — options pool population-level effects treatment-arm pre-baseline scale. controls arm's data directly informs per-study baseline's posterior, can matter one arm substantially direct data . two options statistically equivalent well-identified problems. robust_heterogeneity Logical. TRUE, study-level treatment effects drawn Student-t distribution rather normal, providing robustness outlier studies. degrees--freedom parameter estimated prior specified priors$nu. design_effects Logical. TRUE, additive offsets population treatment effect mean estimated RCT Pre-Post studies relative (reference). Useful testing whether designs yield systematically different effect estimates. hierarchical_rho Logical. TRUE (default), pre-post correlation modelled hierarchically across studies. Studies reported correlation inform population distribution; studies without one correlation imputed. correlated_effects Logical. TRUE, study-level treatment effects time trends drawn jointly bivariate normal shared correlation parameter, rather independently. correlation parameterised via Cholesky factor 2×2 correlation matrix LKJ prior (see set_priors()). combined robust_heterogeneity = TRUE. Default FALSE. baseline_imbalance handle per-study baseline difference \(\gamma_i\) non-designs. studies always estimate \(\gamma_i\) per-study pre-treatment means arms identify directly data; argument controls RCT branch. One : "estimated" (Default) RCT studies also per-study baseline_difference_i parameters, drawn hierarchical prior shared studies. RCT data identify per-study \(\gamma_i\) alone (one post-treatment observation per arm), hierarchical prior \-\- informed 's per-study estimates \-\- pins decomposition baseline imbalance treatment effect. Priors population hyperparameters set via baseline_difference_mean baseline_difference_sd set_priors(). "fixed_zero" Fix \(\gamma_i = 0\) RCT studies. treatment-arm baseline constrained equal control-arm baseline. Use randomisation can assumed eliminate baseline imbalances. studies unaffected. covariates optional one-sided formula specifying study-level covariates meta-regression treatment effect (e.g., ~ dose + year). named columns must numeric present summary_data individual_data (whichever provided). individual-level data, covariate values must constant within study. Default NULL (meta-regression). center_covariates Logical. TRUE (default), covariates mean-centered across studies fitting. ensures treatment_effect_mean population treatment effect average covariate values. Set FALSE use raw covariate values, case treatment_effect_mean effect covariates equal zero. covariate coefficients (beta_cov) interpretation regardless centering: change expected treatment effect per unit increase covariate. priors did_priors object set_priors(). Controls prior distributions population-level parameters. method Inference method. "sample" (default) runs full MCMC via Stan's HMC-NUTS sampler returns posterior distribution. "optimize" finds maximum posteriori (MAP) estimate via L-BFGS substantially faster, returns point estimate uncertainty quantification. chains Number MCMC chains. Ignored method = "optimize". Default 4. iter_warmup Number warmup iterations per chain. Ignored method = "optimize". Default 1000. iter_sampling Number sampling iterations per chain. Ignored method = "optimize". Default 1000. seed Integer random seed reproducibility. Default NULL. allow_no_did Logical. FALSE (default), meta_did() stop error studies present, treatment effect identified data without double-difference structure. Set TRUE override check understand limitation (posterior prior-driven). ... Additional arguments passed underlying CmdStanModel method: $sample() method = "sample" (e.g., parallel_chains, adapt_delta) $optimize() method = "optimize" (e.g., algorithm, iter).

title: Fit a meta-analysis model with explicit control over design assumptions — meta_did_general
text: flexible interface controlling nuisance parameters handled non-study designs. default, behaves identically meta_did(). Users can independently control whether time trends baseline imbalances estimated fixed zero RCT pre-post studies.

title: Fit a meta-analysis model with explicit control over design assumptions — meta_did_general
code: meta_did_general(   summary_data = NULL,   individual_data = NULL,   normalise = TRUE,   baseline_latent_arm = c("treatment", "control"),   robust_heterogeneity = FALSE,   design_effects = FALSE,   hierarchical_rho = TRUE,   correlated_effects = FALSE,   baseline_imbalance = c("estimated", "fixed_zero"),   covariates = NULL,   center_covariates = TRUE,   priors = set_priors(),   time_trend = c("pooled", "fixed_zero"),   pp_likelihood = c("differenced", "bivariate"),   method = c("sample", "optimize"),   chains = 4L,   iter_warmup = 1000L,   iter_sampling = 1000L,   seed = NULL,   allow_no_did = FALSE,   ... )

title: Fit a meta-analysis model with explicit control over design assumptions — meta_did_general
text: summary_data data frame one row per study containing summary statistics. Must include columns study_id design. See validate_summary_data() full column specification per design. Valid designs: "", "did_change", "rct", "pp". individual_data data frame long format one row per observation. Must include columns study_id, design, group, time, value. Valid designs: "", "rct", "pp". study_id may appear summary_data individual_data. normalise Logical. TRUE (default), population-level effects (treatment_effect_mean, time_trend_mean, baseline_difference_mean) expressed fractions treatment-arm pre-treatment baseline. Stan receives raw data unchanged performs normalisation internally via per-study latent baseline parameters. FALSE, per-study latent baseline fit; population-level effects pooled absolute (user-units) scale instead. Equivalent legacy normalise_by_baseline = FALSE behaviour. baseline_latent_arm Character. Advanced. used normalise = TRUE. Determines arm's pre-treatment baseline per-study latent parameter (one wide data-vague uniform prior); arm's baseline derived via hierarchical baseline-difference parameter. One : "treatment" (default): treatment-arm pre-baseline latent, informed directly mean_pre_treatment observations. "control": control-arm pre-baseline latent, informed directly mean_pre_control observations. choice change canonical scale effects reported — options pool population-level effects treatment-arm pre-baseline scale. controls arm's data directly informs per-study baseline's posterior, can matter one arm substantially direct data . two options statistically equivalent well-identified problems. robust_heterogeneity Logical. TRUE, study-level treatment effects drawn Student-t distribution rather normal, providing robustness outlier studies. degrees--freedom parameter estimated prior specified priors$nu. design_effects Logical. TRUE, additive offsets population treatment effect mean estimated RCT Pre-Post studies relative (reference). Useful testing whether designs yield systematically different effect estimates. hierarchical_rho Logical. TRUE (default), pre-post correlation modelled hierarchically across studies. Studies reported correlation inform population distribution; studies without one correlation imputed. correlated_effects Logical. TRUE, study-level treatment effects time trends drawn jointly bivariate normal shared correlation parameter, rather independently. correlation parameterised via Cholesky factor 2×2 correlation matrix LKJ prior (see set_priors()). combined robust_heterogeneity = TRUE. Default FALSE. baseline_imbalance handle baseline difference \(\gamma_i\) treatment control groups RCT studies. studies always estimate \(\gamma_i\) per-study (pre-treatment means identify data). One : "estimated" (Default) Estimate per-study \(\gamma_i\) RCT, drawing hierarchical prior shared 's per-study estimates. behaviour meta_did(). "fixed_zero" Fix \(\gamma_i = 0\) RCT studies, assuming randomisation eliminates baseline imbalances. covariates optional one-sided formula specifying study-level covariates meta-regression treatment effect (e.g., ~ dose + year). named columns must numeric present summary_data individual_data (whichever provided). individual-level data, covariate values must constant within study. Default NULL (meta-regression). center_covariates Logical. TRUE (default), covariates mean-centered across studies fitting. ensures treatment_effect_mean population treatment effect average covariate values. Set FALSE use raw covariate values, case treatment_effect_mean effect covariates equal zero. covariate coefficients (beta_cov) interpretation regardless centering: change expected treatment effect per unit increase covariate. priors did_priors object set_priors(). Controls prior distributions population-level parameters. time_trend handle time trend \(\beta_i\) non-studies. One : "pooled" (Default) Estimate study-level time trends hierarchical prior shared across designs. Information studies informs RCT pre-post time trends. behaviour meta_did(). "fixed_zero" Fix \(\beta_i = 0\) RCT pre-post studies. pre-post studies, means pre-post change attributed entirely treatment. RCT studies, reparameterised time trend correction bypassed. pp_likelihood Likelihood form pre-post studies. One : "differenced" (Default) Use differenced (post minus pre) likelihood. pre/post correlation \(\rho_i\) separately estimable baseline cancels algebraically. behaviour meta_did(). "bivariate" Use full bivariate normal likelihood (pre, post) pair. retains pre/post correlation \(\rho_i\) estimable parameter, contributing hierarchical \(\rho\) model, cost estimating additional nuisance parameters. method Inference method. "sample" (default) runs full MCMC via Stan's HMC-NUTS sampler returns posterior distribution. "optimize" finds maximum posteriori (MAP) estimate via L-BFGS substantially faster, returns point estimate uncertainty quantification. chains Number MCMC chains. Ignored method = "optimize". Default 4. iter_warmup Number warmup iterations per chain. Ignored method = "optimize". Default 1000. iter_sampling Number sampling iterations per chain. Ignored method = "optimize". Default 1000. seed Integer random seed reproducibility. Default NULL. allow_no_did Logical. FALSE (default), meta_did() stop error studies present, treatment effect identified data without double-difference structure. Set TRUE override check understand limitation (posterior prior-driven). ... Additional arguments passed underlying CmdStanModel method: $sample() method = "sample" (e.g., parallel_chains, adapt_delta) $optimize() method = "optimize" (e.g., algorithm, iter).

what: Fit a naive meta-analysis model (no borrowing across designs) — meta_did_naive
title: Fit a naive meta-analysis model (no borrowing across designs) — meta_did_naive
text: Deprecated. meta_did_naive() deprecated favour meta_did_general() time_trend = "fixed_zero" baseline_imbalance = "fixed_zero", provides behaviour explicit control. function imposes "naive" assumptions typically made analysing design isolation: RCT: Baseline means assumed equal across treatment control groups (randomisation assumption), baseline difference \(\gamma\) fixed zero. Pre-post: time trend \(\beta\) fixed zero, pre-post change attributed entirely treatment effect.

title: Fit a naive meta-analysis model (no borrowing across designs) — meta_did_naive
code: meta_did_naive(   summary_data = NULL,   individual_data = NULL,   normalise = TRUE,   baseline_latent_arm = c("treatment", "control"),   robust_heterogeneity = FALSE,   design_effects = FALSE,   hierarchical_rho = TRUE,   covariates = NULL,   center_covariates = TRUE,   priors = set_priors(),   method = c("sample", "optimize"),   chains = 4L,   iter_warmup = 1000L,   iter_sampling = 1000L,   seed = NULL,   allow_no_did = FALSE,   ... )

title: Fit a naive meta-analysis model (no borrowing across designs) — meta_did_naive
text: summary_data data frame one row per study containing summary statistics. Must include columns study_id design. See validate_summary_data() full column specification per design. Valid designs: "", "did_change", "rct", "pp". individual_data data frame long format one row per observation. Must include columns study_id, design, group, time, value. Valid designs: "", "rct", "pp". study_id may appear summary_data individual_data. normalise Logical. TRUE (default), population-level effects (treatment_effect_mean, time_trend_mean, baseline_difference_mean) expressed fractions treatment-arm pre-treatment baseline. Stan receives raw data unchanged performs normalisation internally via per-study latent baseline parameters. FALSE, per-study latent baseline fit; population-level effects pooled absolute (user-units) scale instead. Equivalent legacy normalise_by_baseline = FALSE behaviour. baseline_latent_arm Character. Advanced. used normalise = TRUE. Determines arm's pre-treatment baseline per-study latent parameter (one wide data-vague uniform prior); arm's baseline derived via hierarchical baseline-difference parameter. One : "treatment" (default): treatment-arm pre-baseline latent, informed directly mean_pre_treatment observations. "control": control-arm pre-baseline latent, informed directly mean_pre_control observations. choice change canonical scale effects reported — options pool population-level effects treatment-arm pre-baseline scale. controls arm's data directly informs per-study baseline's posterior, can matter one arm substantially direct data . two options statistically equivalent well-identified problems. robust_heterogeneity Logical. TRUE, study-level treatment effects drawn Student-t distribution rather normal, providing robustness outlier studies. degrees--freedom parameter estimated prior specified priors$nu. design_effects Logical. TRUE, additive offsets population treatment effect mean estimated RCT Pre-Post studies relative (reference). Useful testing whether designs yield systematically different effect estimates. hierarchical_rho Logical. TRUE (default), pre-post correlation modelled hierarchically across studies. Studies reported correlation inform population distribution; studies without one correlation imputed. covariates optional one-sided formula specifying study-level covariates meta-regression treatment effect (e.g., ~ dose + year). named columns must numeric present summary_data individual_data (whichever provided). individual-level data, covariate values must constant within study. Default NULL (meta-regression). center_covariates Logical. TRUE (default), covariates mean-centered across studies fitting. ensures treatment_effect_mean population treatment effect average covariate values. Set FALSE use raw covariate values, case treatment_effect_mean effect covariates equal zero. covariate coefficients (beta_cov) interpretation regardless centering: change expected treatment effect per unit increase covariate. priors did_priors object set_priors(). Controls prior distributions population-level parameters. method Inference method. "sample" (default) runs full MCMC via Stan's HMC-NUTS sampler returns posterior distribution. "optimize" finds maximum posteriori (MAP) estimate via L-BFGS substantially faster, returns point estimate uncertainty quantification. chains Number MCMC chains. Ignored method = "optimize". Default 4. iter_warmup Number warmup iterations per chain. Ignored method = "optimize". Default 1000. iter_sampling Number sampling iterations per chain. Ignored method = "optimize". Default 1000. seed Integer random seed reproducibility. Default NULL. allow_no_did Logical. FALSE (default), meta_did() stop error studies present, treatment effect identified data without double-difference structure. Set TRUE override check understand limitation (posterior prior-driven). ... Additional arguments passed underlying CmdStanModel method: $sample() method = "sample" (e.g., parallel_chains, adapt_delta) $optimize() method = "optimize" (e.g., algorithm, iter).

title: Set prior distributions for the meta-analysis model — set_priors
text: treatment_effect_mean Prior population treatment effect mean. Default: normal(0, 10). treatment_effect_sd Prior -study SD. Default: cauchy(5). time_trend_mean Prior population time-trend mean. Default: normal(0, 10). time_trend_sd Prior -study time-trend SD. Default: cauchy(5). rho_mean Prior mean Fisher-z transformed pre-post correlation (used hierarchical_rho = TRUE). Default: normal(0, 1). rho_sd Prior SD Fisher-z transformed pre-post correlation (used hierarchical_rho = TRUE). Default: normal(0, 0.5). nu Prior degrees freedom -study heterogeneity (used robust_heterogeneity = TRUE). Default: gamma(2, 0.1). delta_rct Prior RCT design offset relative (used design_effects = TRUE). Default: normal(0, 10). delta_pp Prior Pre-Post design offset relative (used design_effects = TRUE). Default: normal(0, 10). sigma Prior study-level observation standard deviations (shared across designs). Default: cauchy(5). beta_cov Prior covariate regression coefficients (used covariates specified meta_did()). Default: normal(0, 10). lkj_eta Prior Cholesky factor correlation matrix treatment effects time trends (used correlated_effects = TRUE). Default: lkj(2), gently regularises toward zero correlation. baseline_difference_mean Prior population mean per-study baseline imbalance \(\delta_i = (b_{T,} - b_{C,}) / b_{C,}\), defined fractional difference treatment-arm control-arm pre-treatment baselines, expressed fraction control-arm baseline (control-pre reference convention). Constrained \(\delta_i > -1\) derived \((1 + \delta_i)\) factor remains positive modelled-mode parameterisations. used baseline_imbalance = "estimated". Default: normal(0, 0.5). baseline_difference_sd Prior -study SD baseline imbalance. used baseline_imbalance = "estimated". Default: cauchy(0.1).

text: did_priors object.

code: simulate_meta_did(   n_studies = 20L,   n_control = 100L,   n_treatment = 100L,   true_effect = -0.15,   sigma_effect = 0.03,   true_trend = -0.02,   sigma_trend = 0,   baseline_mean = 0.45,   baseline_sd = 0,   within_sd = 0.12,   rho = 0.5,   rho_effect_trend = 0,   seed = NULL,   covariates = NULL,   beta_cov = NULL )

title: Simulate individual-level DiD data for a collection of studies — simulate_meta_did
text: n_studies Number studies. Default 20. n_control Number individuals per study control arm. Default 100L. n_treatment Number individuals per study treatment arm. Default 100L. true_effect Population mean treatment effect. Default -0.15. sigma_effect -study SD treatment effects. Default 0.03. true_trend Population mean time trend. Default -0.02. sigma_trend -study SD time trends. Default 0. baseline_mean Population mean study baselines. Default 0.45. baseline_sd -study SD baselines. Default 0. within_sd Individual-level SD (pre post groups). Default 0.12. rho Pre-post correlation within individuals. Directly parameterises -diagonal bivariate normal covariance matrix. Default 0.5. rho_effect_trend Correlation study-level treatment effects \(\theta_i\) time trends \(\beta_i\). nonzero (sigma_effect sigma_trend positive), pair drawn jointly bivariate normal. Default 0 (independent draws). seed Integer random seed reproducibility. Default NULL. covariates optional data frame n_studies rows containing study-level covariate values. Column names used covariate names. Default NULL (covariates). beta_cov numeric vector true covariate regression coefficients, length equal ncol(covariates). Required covariates provided. coefficient represents change treatment effect per unit increase corresponding covariate. Default NULL.

title: Simulate individual-level DiD data for a collection of studies — simulate_meta_did
text: data frame columns study_id, subject_id, group ("control" "treatment"), time ("pre" "post"), value, plus covariate columns (repeated observation within study). true study-level parameters attached attribute "true_params".

title: Simulate individual-level DiD data for a collection of studies — simulate_meta_did
text: Study-level parameters drawn hierarchically: $$\theta_i \sim \text{Normal}(\texttt{true\_effect},\, \texttt{sigma\_effect}^2)$$ $$\beta_i \sim \text{Normal}(\texttt{true\_trend},\, \texttt{sigma\_trend}^2)$$ $$b_i      \sim \text{Normal}(\texttt{baseline\_mean},\, \texttt{baseline\_sd}^2)$$ rho_effect_trend nonzero, \((\theta_i, \beta_i)\) drawn jointly bivariate normal correlation rho_effect_trend. Within study, individual (pre, post) pairs drawn bivariate normal distribution covariance matrix $$\Sigma = \sigma^2 \begin{pmatrix} 1 & \rho \\ \rho & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$ making role \(\rho\) pre-post correlation explicit. mean vector control group \((b_i,\, b_i + \beta_i)\) treatment group \((b_i,\, b_i + \beta_i + \theta_i)\).

title: Simulate individual-level DiD data for a collection of studies — simulate_meta_did
code: dat <- simulate_meta_did(n_studies = 10, true_effect = -0.15, seed = 42) head(dat) #> # A tibble: 6 × 5 #>   study_id subject_id group   time  value #>   <chr>         <int> <chr>   <chr> <dbl> #> 1 study_1           1 control pre   0.607 #> 2 study_1           1 control post  0.506 #> 3 study_1           2 control pre   0.724 #> 4 study_1           2 control post  0.578 #> 5 study_1           3 control pre   0.283 #> 6 study_1           3 control post  0.296 attr(dat, "true_params") #> # A tibble: 10 × 4 #>    study_id   theta  beta baseline #>    <chr>      <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl> #>  1 study_1  -0.109  -0.02     0.45 #>  2 study_2  -0.167  -0.02     0.45 #>  3 study_3  -0.139  -0.02     0.45 #>  4 study_4  -0.131  -0.02     0.45 #>  5 study_5  -0.138  -0.02     0.45 #>  6 study_6  -0.153  -0.02     0.45 #>  7 study_7  -0.105  -0.02     0.45 #>  8 study_8  -0.153  -0.02     0.45 #>  9 study_9  -0.0894 -0.02     0.45 #> 10 study_10 -0.152  -0.02     0.45
